does: This paper introduces a distributional framework for visual mechanistic interpretability by modeling how feature activations influence the natural image distribution and formulating this as a KL-minimization problem. The authors identify theoretical biases in existing interpretability methods—namely that heuristic approaches like top-K activation retrieval and regularized optimization produce either perceptually unnatural images or fail to faithfully activate model features. To address these limitations, they propose a KL-minimal soft-constraint principle that theoretically balances interpretability and faithfulness, implementing this approach through energy-guided diffusion posterior sampling. Experimental validation demonstrates that their method generates more interpretable and faithful visualizations compared to existing paradigms.
connects: This work builds on the established field of visual mechanistic interpretability, which seeks to understand internal representations in vision models through visualization and attribution methods. It extends prior heuristic approaches including top-K activation maximization and regularization-based feature visualization by grounding these methods in distributional theory. The paper responds to fundamental tensions in existing MI work between producing human-interpretable visualizations and maintaining mechanistic faithfulness to actual model computations. It leverages recent advances in diffusion models and energy-based sampling, connecting to the broader literature on generative models for inverse problems and posterior sampling, while also engaging with information-theoretic principles for balancing competing objectives.
enables: This distributional framework opens new directions for mechanistic interpretability by providing a principled theoretical foundation for balancing interpretability against model faithfulness. The KL-minimal soft-constraint approach could inspire further development of theoretically-grounded visualization methods beyond vision, extending to other modalities and architectures. The work enables better diagnostic tools for understanding failure modes in vision systems and could facilitate more trustworthy AI development by providing clearer windows into model behavior. Future work might leverage this framework to design interventions that modify learned representations, develop more efficient sampling procedures for scaled interpretability, or extend the approach to multi-layer and circuit-level interpretability analysis.
